target_exercise.context: Countries A and B from §4.3.1 each have 3000 labor hours, split evenly before trade. Terms of trade proposed: 1 car = 8 tons of grain.

subquestions.label: a
subquestions.prompt: Calculate production of each good in each country before trade.

subquestions.label: b
subquestions.prompt: Now each country fully specialises. Calculate total world production of each good.

subquestions.label: c
subquestions.prompt: Show that world production has increased for at least one good (without decreasing the other).

subquestions.label: d
subquestions.prompt: Country A exports grain to B and imports cars. The terms of trade are: 1 car = 8 tons of grain. With trade, A consumes 15 cars and 185 tons of grain. Is A better off than before trade? Show with numbers.

subquestions.label: e
subquestions.prompt: At which terms of trade would A refuse to trade? At which would B refuse? (Hint: use opportunity costs from §4.3.1.)

lesson_goals: Calculate production before and after specialisation
Show that specialisation increases total world output
Define terms of trade: the exchange ratio between goods
Determine the range of mutually beneficial terms of trade (between the two opportunity costs)
Understand that both countries can gain from trade
missing_units_flagged: Compute pre-trade and post-specialisation production for each country and quantify the world-output gain (A-domain candidate)
Define terms-of-trade as the exchange ratio between two goods and compare consumption bundles before vs after trade (concept-level + reken)
Determine the mutually-beneficial range of terms-of-trade from the two countries' opportunity costs (concept-level; the bounded-interval reasoning)
exercises.difficulty_notes: Lots of arithmetic but conceptually straightforward once comparative advantage is understood. Exercise (e) requires connecting back to opportunity costs — elegant but potentially confusing. One lesson works if §4.3.1 was solid. If not, this spills.

target_exercise.context: Domestic market: Qv = −2P + 200, Qa = P − 10 (P in €, Q in thousands of units). World price €40. Government imposes a €10 tariff raising the import price to €50.

subquestions.label: a
subquestions.prompt: At free trade: calculate domestic Qd, domestic Qa, and imports (Qd − Qa).

subquestions.label: b
subquestions.prompt: The government imposes a tariff of €10. The new import price is €50. Calculate the new domestic Qd, Qa, and imports.

subquestions.label: c
subquestions.prompt: Calculate the tariff revenue for the government.

subquestions.label: d
subquestions.prompt: Draw the free-trade and post-tariff situations. Shade the two deadweight loss triangles.

subquestions.label: e
subquestions.prompt: Who benefits from the tariff? Who loses? Evaluate: 'Tariffs protect domestic jobs.'

lesson_goals: Calculate import quantity under free trade (domestic demand − domestic supply at world price)
Analyse the effect of a tariff: higher price, lower imports, higher domestic production
Calculate tariff revenue
Identify welfare effects: domestic producers gain, consumers lose, government gains revenue, net DWL
Evaluate protectionist arguments critically
missing_units_flagged: Compute imports = domestic Qd − domestic Qa at a given world price (A-domain candidate; the free-trade baseline calculation)
Critically evaluate 'tariffs protect domestic jobs' using winners-vs-losers surplus accounting (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling)
exercises.difficulty_notes: The graph has two DWL triangles (production inefficiency + consumption loss) — this is more complex than the single DWL triangle from taxes. However, the calculation itself is straightforward (just reading off demand and supply at two prices). The welfare analysis connects directly to Module 2's tax analysis. One lesson works because it's application, not new methodology.

target_exercise.context: A shopping basket costs €150 in 2023 (base year, index = 100) and €162 in 2025. In 2026 the CPI is 112. A worker's nominal wage rises from €3000/month in 2023 to €3200 in 2025.

subquestions.label: a
subquestions.prompt: Calculate the CPI for 2025.

subquestions.label: b
subquestions.prompt: Calculate the inflation rate from 2023 to 2025.

subquestions.label: c
subquestions.prompt: In 2026, the CPI is 112. Calculate the inflation from 2025 to 2026. (Watch out: not 4%!)

subquestions.label: d
subquestions.prompt: A worker's nominal wage rises from €3000/month in 2023 to €3200 in 2025. Calculate the real wage change. Did the worker's purchasing power increase or decrease?

subquestions.label: e
subquestions.prompt: Explain the difference between nominal and real values. Why do economists prefer to use real values?

lesson_goals: Calculate CPI = (basket cost in year t / basket cost in base year) × 100
Calculate inflation rate from CPI values
Distinguish between index point changes and percentage changes (critical exam skill!)
Calculate real values: real wage = nominal wage / CPI × 100
Explain why real values correct for inflation
missing_units_flagged: Compute real wage = nominal wage / CPI × 100 and interpret the direction (A-domain candidate; I09 is close but macro-framed)
Explain why economists prefer real values (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
exercises.difficulty_notes: Exercise (c) is the classic trap — students think '108 to 112 = 4% inflation' when it's actually (112−108)/108 = 3.7%. This connects directly to §1.1.2 (percentage changes). The real vs nominal distinction is conceptually important but mathematically simple. One lesson works.

target_exercise.context: A pension of €1500/month. Inflation 4%, average wage growth 6%. Students compare three indexering regimes: waardevast, welvaartsvast, bevroren.

subquestions.label: a
subquestions.prompt: The pension is waardevast (indexed to inflation). Calculate the new pension.

subquestions.label: b
subquestions.prompt: The pension is welvaartsvast (indexed to wage growth). Calculate the new pension.

subquestions.label: c
subquestions.prompt: The pension is bevroren (frozen). Calculate the real purchasing power loss in euros per month.

subquestions.label: d
subquestions.prompt: Which indexering method is best for the pensioner? Which is cheapest for the pension fund? Explain the trade-off.

subquestions.label: e
subquestions.prompt: A country experiences 2% deflation. What happens to the purchasing power of a frozen pension? Is this good or bad for the pensioner?

lesson_goals: Define waardevast (indexed to CPI/inflation) vs welvaartsvast (indexed to wages)
Calculate pensions/wages after indexering
Calculate purchasing power changes when pensions are frozen
Understand the trade-off between protecting pensioners and pension fund sustainability
Apply concepts to deflation (reverse situation)
missing_units_flagged: Define waardevast (indexed to inflation) vs welvaartsvast (indexed to wage growth) vs bevroren (no indexering) — pensioen-indexering vocab (concept-level, mastery_target understand)
Compute the new pension under each of the three indexering regimes and compute the real purchasing-power loss under bevroren (A-domain candidate)
Evaluate the pensioner-vs-fund trade-off across the three regimes (concept-level + verbal; exam standpuntbepaling prep)
Apply the purchasing-power logic under deflation (concept-level; reversed-sign case, good diagnostic)
exercises.difficulty_notes: The terminology (waardevast, welvaartsvast, bevroren) is confusing because the words sound similar. Exercises (a–c) force precise calculation for each case. Exercise (e) tests deeper understanding. One lesson works — the math is arithmetic, the challenge is keeping the definitions straight.

exercises.paragraph_title: Real vs nominal: integration and application

target_exercise.context: Integration lesson applying the real vs nominal distinction to interest rates, GDP growth, wages, and central bank policy.

subquestions.label: a
subquestions.prompt: The nominal interest rate on a savings account is 5%. Inflation is 3%. Calculate the real interest rate (use: real ≈ nominal − inflation). If you save €10,000, how much more can you actually buy after one year?

subquestions.label: b
subquestions.prompt: Country X's nominal GDP grew from €800 billion to €840 billion. The GDP deflator rose from 100 to 103. Calculate real GDP growth. Did the country produce more goods and services, or did prices just rise?

subquestions.label: c
subquestions.prompt: A worker's nominal wage rises from €2500 to €2600 (4% increase). Inflation is 5%. Calculate the real wage change. Is this worker better or worse off?

subquestions.label: d
subquestions.prompt: In the 1970s, inflation in the Netherlands was 10% and nominal wages rose by 8%. Explain what happened to workers' purchasing power. How might this affect consumption?

subquestions.label: e
subquestions.prompt: Combine your knowledge: explain why a central bank targets low inflation (around 2%). Use the concepts real interest rate, purchasing power, and wage earners in your answer.

lesson_goals: Calculate real interest rate ≈ nominal interest rate − inflation
Calculate real GDP growth using the GDP deflator
Apply the real vs nominal framework to wages, interest rates, and GDP
Understand why persistent inflation erodes purchasing power
Explain why central banks target low inflation (integration question)
missing_units_flagged: Compute real interest rate ≈ nominal − inflation and interpret purchasing-power effect on savings (concept-level + reken; partial overlap with H15 which is macro-framed)
Compute real GDP growth from nominal GDP change and GDP deflator change (A-domain candidate; the deflator-adjustment mechanic)
Explain why central banks target ~2% inflation, connecting real interest rate, purchasing power, and wage-earners (concept-level + verbal; exam integration question — the §4.4.3 payoff)
exercises.difficulty_notes: This is an integration lesson — no new concepts, just applying the real/nominal distinction from §4.4.1 and the indexering concepts from §4.4.2 to different economic variables. Exercise (e) is a synthesis question that prepares for exam-style evaluative answers. One lesson comfortable.
